title: File Formats, Parquet & DuckDB
text:
● Data Storage Essentials
    File Formats,Parquet &DuckDB
    How data is stored on disk shapes how fast you can query it along with the space it takes up on the disk. From row-based CSVs to columnar Parquet files to embedded analytical databases every format has benefits and trade-offs.
    
      Storage Layouts
      Parquet Internals
      DuckDB Architecture
      When to Use What
    
    Scroll to explore↓
  




  01
  
    Foundations
    File formats & storage layouts
    Before writing a single query, the data has to live somewhere. How a file arranges its bytes on disk — row by row, column by column, or a hybrid of both — determines what kinds of queries run fast and what kinds run slow.
  





  

    
    
      
        Storage layout comparison
        

          
          
             Row-Based (CSV, JSON)
            
              A1B1C1D1
              A2B2C2D2
              A3B3C3D3
            
            All columns per row stored together on disk
          

          
          
            Columnar (Pure Columnar)
            
              A1A2A3A4
              B1B2B3B4
              C1C2C3C4
              D1D2D3D4
            
            Each column stored contiguously — read only what you need
          

          
          
            Hybrid (Parquet) — Row Groups
            
              Row Group 1
              
                A1A2
                B1B2
                C1C2
                D1D2
              
              Row Group 2
              
                A3A4
                B3B4
                C3C4
                D3D4
              
            
            Rows chunked into groups; within each group, stored columnarly
          

        
      
    

    
    
      
        Format comparison
        



Feature
CSV
JSON
Parquet




Layout
Row
Row
Hybrid


Human-readable
Yes
Yes
No


Built-in compression
No
No
Yes


Schema embedded
No
Partial
Yes


Query efficiency
Low
Low
High


Typical file size
Medium
Large
Small


Best for
Simple exports
APIs / config
Analytics




      
    

    
    
      
        Relative file size — same dataset
        
          
            JSON
            
              1,834,260 bytes
            
          
          
            CSV
            
              829,667 bytes
            
          
          
            Parquet
            
              327,738 bytes
            
          
        
        
          Why is Parquet so much smaller?
          
            Columnar storage groups similar values together, making compression extremely effective. Parquet also uses dictionary encoding, replacing repeated values like "California" with a tiny integer code across all occurrences.
          
        
        
          
            JSON
            1×
            baseline
          
          
            CSV
            2.2×
            smaller
          
          
            Parquet
            5.6×
            smaller
          
        
      
    

  

  

    
      1.1 · Storage Layouts
      How a file stores its bytes changes everything
      A main distinction between file storage tupes is how the data is grouped, mainlyrow by row or column by column. This different impacts what kinds of queries and tasks perform more efficiently.
      A row-based format like CSV or JSON stores all of a record's fields together. Reading one row is fast, but reading a single column across millions of rows means touching every row's data on disk, even the columns you don't care about.
      A columnar format stores all values from one column together. Analytical queries that compute aggregates over one or two columns out of dozens skip the irrelevant columns entirely, the disk reads are far smaller and thus more efficient.
      Parquet uses a hybrid: it partitions rows into large row groups (typically 100K–1M rows each) for large datasets, and within each group stores data columnarly. This gives columnar compression benefits while keeping related rows together for efficient scanning.
      
        Row-based
        Columnar
        Hybrid
        Row Groups
      
    

    
      1.2 · Formats Compared
      CSV, JSON, and Parquet serve different purposes
      CSV is the simplest and typically most standard file type. It is a plain-text file where every value is separated by a comma representing each row at a time. Anyone can open it in a spreadsheet and read it. But it has no schema, no type information, no compression, and the database has to infer everything.
      JSON is more expressive, it supports nested objects and arrays, making it ideal for APIs and configuration. But nesting means the structure is irregular, which makes bulk analysis slow and produces large files. JSON is the least efficient storage format for analytics and typically the largest in size.
      Parquet is purpose-built for analytical workloads and storing information efficiently (compactly). It is not human-readable, in binary format but it stores the full schema in its footer, supports built-in column-level compression, and enables query engines to skip entire row groups that can't match a filter using stored statistics (located in the footer of the file).
      
        CSV: simple & portable
        JSON: flexible & verbose
        Parquet: fast & compact
      
    

    
      1.3 · Compression & Encoding
      Columnar storage compresses information more efficiently
      The graphic on the left comes from a real experiment comparing the same dataset stored in all three formats. Each format stores the exact same information on Stanford Open Policing project information from Los Angelese. However, the difference is striking in file size: the Parquet file is over 5× smaller than JSON and nearly 2.5× smaller than CSV.
      This happens for two reasons. First, columnar grouping means similar values sit next to each other in the file, compression algorithms like Snappy or Zstd exploit this repetition extremely well. Second, Parquet uses dictionary encoding: if a string column contains the same 50 city names repeated millions of times, Parquet replaces each string with a compact integer and stores a single dictionary. The savings compound to make a strong disparity.
      Smaller files mean fewer bytes read from disk, which is usually the primary bottleneck in data analysis. Parquet's compression is not just a storage benefit, it is a query performance benefit too.
      
        Dictionary encoding
        Snappy / Zstd compression
        Less disk I/O
        Schema in footer
      
    

  




  
    Fun historical insight
    Parquet was actually made by Twitter, and Google aided in its creation.
    Parquet's origin story actually traces back to a 2010 Google research paper called "Dremel: Interactive Analysis of Web-Scale Datasets", which introduced the idea of representing nested data in a columnar format. This trick let Google query massive datasets interactively instead of waiting on slow batch jobs.Three years later, in 2013, two unlikely co-conspirators Twitter and Cloudera joined forces and built Parquet together and donated it to the Apache Software Foundation, making it free and open for anyone to use. Twitter needed to crunch enormous volumes of data fast, and Cloudera was deep in the business of helping companies do exactly that at scale. Today Parquet is widely known as a open source file storage format purpose-built for analytical OLAP-style workloads, heavy on reads and aggregations, over huge piles of data, and it's remarkably space-efficient.
  




  02
  
    Deep Dive
    Inside a Parquet file
    Parquet's speed comes from its internal structure. Understanding how a Parquet file is laid out on disk explains why query engines can skip irrelevant data without reading it.
  





  

    
    
      
        Internal layout of a .parquet file
        
          PAR1  (magic bytes — identifies Parquet format)

          
          
            Row Group 1  (e.g. rows 0 – 999,999)
            
              
                Col A
                Data Page
                Dict Page
                Data Page
              
              
                Col B
                Data Page
                Dict Page
                Data Page
              
              
                Col C
                Data Page
                Dict Page
                Data Page
              
            
          

          
          
            Row Group 2  (e.g. rows 1,000,000 – 1,999,999)
            
              
                Col A
                Data Page
                Dict Page
              
              
                Col B
                Data Page
                Dict Page
              
              
                Col C
                Data Page
                Dict Page
              
            
          

          
            FOOTER — schema, row group byte offsets, statistics (min/max per column per row group), encodings
          
          PAR1  (magic bytes — end of file)
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Row group skipping in action

        Query: WHERE date > '2023-06-01'

        
        
          
          
            
              Row Group 1
              date: 2021-01-01 → 2021-12-31
            
            ⏭ SKIPPED
          
          
            
              Row Group 2
              date: 2022-01-01 → 2022-12-31
            
            ⏭ SKIPPED
          
          
            
              Row Group 3
              date: 2023-01-01 → 2023-05-30
            
            ⏭ SKIPPED
          
          
          
            
              Row Group 4
              date: 2023-06-01 → 2023-12-31
            
            ✓ READ
          
          
            
              Row Group 5
              date: 2024-01-01 → 2024-12-31
            
            ✓ READ
          
        

        
          The footer's per-row-group min/max statistics let the engine skip 3 of 5 row groups before reading a single data byte.
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Parquet advantages
        

          
            
            
              Columnar grouped storage
              Reads only the columns needed: analytical queries touch a fraction of the data
            
          

          
            
            
              Compression efficiency
              Built-in Snappy / Zstd compression: Parquet files are consistently 3–10× smaller than equivalent CSVs
            
          

          
            
            
              Dictionary encoding
              Replaces repeated string values with compact integer codes: huge savings on categorical columns
            
          

          
            
            
              Schema embedded in footer
              Column names, types, and statistics are stored in the file itself: no external schema needed
            
          

          
            
            
              Schema evolution
              Add or remove columns without rewriting the entire file: new tools can simply ignore unknown columns
            
          

        
      
    

  

  

    
      2.1 · File Structure
      A Parquet file has more than just data
      Every Parquet file starts and ends with the four magic bytes PAR1. This simply identify's the file on the computer as a parquet file. Between them, the data is divided into row groups: horizontal partitions, each containing a chunk of rows. Within each row group, every column's data is stored as its own contiguous column chunk, further split into pages.
      The footer at the end of the file is critical and unique to parquet files compared to JSON or CSV. It holds the full schema (column names and data types), the byte offset of every row group, and per-column statistics including minimum and maximum values. A query engine reads the footer first and uses it to build a plan before reading any actual data.
      This design means a tool like DuckDB can open a Parquet file, read the footer in milliseconds, and immediately know which row groups can be skipped for a given query filter,without touching any data pages.
      
        PAR1 magic bytes
        Row groups
        Column chunks
        Footer metadata
      
    

    
      2.2 · Predicate Pushdown
      Row group skipping and why it is beneficial
      Parquet's footer stores the minimum and maximum value of every column in every row group. This enables a technique called predicate pushdown: pushing a filter condition down into the storage layer so data that can't possibly match is never loaded.
      Consider WHERE date > '2023-06-01' against a file with five row groups. The footer reveals row groups 1–3 have max dates before the cutoff. The engine skips them entirely. Only groups 4 and 5 are read. If you have 100 row groups and the filter eliminates 90, you read 10% of the file.
      This is a form of metadata-driven pruning. It doesn't require a B+ tree index since the statistics in the footer are sufficient. It is one of the main reasons Parquet files can be queried efficiently by tools that don't load the data into a database first.
      
        Predicate pushdown
        Min/max statistics
        Row group pruning
        No index needed
      
    

    
      2.3 · When to Use Parquet
      Parquet Benefits
      Use Parquet when your workflow is analytical. In other words, when your frequent queries aggregate, filter, or summarize across many rows. It outperforms CSV and JSON on these workloads not by a small margin but by an order of magnitude, because it reads far fewer bytes from disk.
      Parquet's universality is also a major advantage. It works seamlessly across the entire modern data stack — Spark, DuckDB, Pandas, Polars, BigQuery, Snowflake, Athena, and Hive all read and write Parquet natively. Choosing Parquet as your storage format means you can utilize many various tools.
      The only reason to prefer CSV or JSON over Parquet is human readability and editability. If someone needs to open the file in a text editor or spreadsheet, CSV would be better. If you're building an API response payload, JSON would be better. If you're storing data for analysis, Parquet is almost always the better choice.
      
        Analytical workloads
        Universal compatibility
        Schema evolution
        Not human-readable
      
    

  




  
    So who actually queries Parquet?
    DuckDB, known as the "SQLite for analytics" is a full SQL engine that runs entirely inside your process, with no server to configure.
    DuckDB can query Parquet files directly on disk with no load step. It combines Parquet's columnar storage efficiency with full SQL expressiveness and the simplicity of an embedded database.
  




  03
  
    Embedded Analytics
    DuckDB
    DuckDB is an open-source, in-process analytical SQL database. It brings the full capabilities of SQL to local data files — CSV, Parquet, JSON — without requiring a server, users, passwords, or any infrastructure.
  





  

    
    
      
        DuckDB — how it fits in your workflow
        
          
            
            
              Your Program (Python / R / CLI)
              You write SQL queries and receive results as data frames or tables
            
          
          ↕ in-process (no network hop)
          
            
            
              DuckDB Engine
              SQL parser → optimizer → vectorized execution engine
            
          
          ↕ reads directly
          
            
            
              Local Files
              Can read parquet files directly, no loading required
            
          
          — or writes to —
          
            
            
              DuckDB Database (.duckdb)
              Single file on disk. Persistent, columnar, compressed storage
            
          
        
        
          No server. No users. No config. Install as a package and start querying.
        
      
    

    
    
      
        DuckDB vs. MySQL vs. PostgreSQL
        



Dimension
MySQL
PostgreSQL
DuckDB




Architecture
Client-server
Client-server
In-process


Optimized for
OLTP
OLTP + OLAP
OLAP


Setup required
Server + config
Server + config
pip/install.packages


Storage layout
Row (B+ tree)
Row (heap)
Columnar


Query files directly
No
No
Yes (CSV/Parquet/JSON)


Best for
Web apps
Complex apps
Local analytics




        
          DuckDB is not a replacement for MySQL or PostgreSQL. It's a different tool for a different job: local analytical queries instead of multi-user transactional applications.
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Querying files directly — no database needed
        
-- Query a Parquet file directly on disk
SELECT
  column_a,
  SUM(column_b) AS total
FROM read_parquet('data.parquet')
WHERE column_a = 'value'
GROUP BY column_a;
-- Query ALL parquet files in a folder
SELECT * FROM read_parquet('folder/*.parquet');
-- Load into a persistent DuckDB database
CREATE TABLE stops AS
  SELECT * FROM read_parquet('*.parquet');
        
        
          DuckDB reads the Parquet footer first, pushes predicates into the scan, and skips irrelevant row groups.
        
      
    

  

  

    
      3.1 · What is DuckDB
      An embedded SQL engine and no server required
      DuckDB is described as "the SQLite for analytics." SQLite is the local SQL dialect and like SQLite, duckdb is an embedded, in-process database. It runs entirely inside your program rather than as a separate server. The entire engine is a single library you install as a package in Python or R. There is no authentication, no port to configure, and no connection to start.
      Unlike SQLite, which is optimized for transactional row-level operations just like MySQL, DuckDB is purpose-built for analytical workloads. It uses a columnar execution engine, processes data in batches, and is optimized for queries that scan many rows, aggregate, group, filter, and join large datasets.
      A DuckDB database is a single file on disk with a .duckdb extension. You can also use DuckDB in fully in-memory mode, where nothing is written to disk at all. Both modes give you full SQL expressiveness with analytical performance.
      
        Embedded / in-process
        No server needed
        Columnar execution
        Single-file database
      
    

    
      3.2 · DuckDB vs. Traditional SQL
      A different kind of SQL database
      MySQL and PostgreSQL are client-server databases: a long-running server process manages shared access, handles concurrent transactions, and stores data in row-oriented storage. This architecture is excellent for web applications with many simultaneous users reading and writing small numbers of rows.
      DuckDB makes the opposite trade-offs. It is designed for a single analyst or process running complex queries over large datasets. Because it runs in-process, there is no network overhead between the query engine and the query. The results come back as in-memory data frames, not network packets like with MySQL or PostgreSQL.
      The storage engine is also fundamentally different. MySQL stores table data in B+ trees (row-oriented). DuckDB stores data columnarly with vectorized execution, processing columns in batches of 2,048 values at a time. This is precisely what makes aggregation queries fast: the CPU can operate on contiguous arrays of values rather than jumping between rows.
      
        Vectorized execution
        No network overhead
        Batch processing
        Single-user analytics
      
    

    
      3.3 · Querying Files Directly
      DuckDB can query Parquet files without loading them first
      One of DuckDB's most powerful features is the ability to run SQL queries directly against parquet files on disk, no database ingestion step required. The read_parquet() function accepts a file path (or a glob pattern like *.parquet) and exposes it as a queryable table.
      This is not just a convenience feature. DuckDB's Parquet reader exploits the file's footer statistics to implement predicate pushdown, the same row group skipping described earlier. A filter in your SQL WHERE clause is pushed all the way into the file scan, skipping row groups that can't match. This means you get analytical warehouse behavior on local files.
      When you do want persistence, the CREATE TABLE AS SELECT pattern copies data into DuckDB's own columnar format, which is even faster for repeated queries. The choice between querying files directly and loading into a native database is a trade-off depending on the query and the frequency.
      
        read_parquet()
        Glob patterns
        Predicate pushdown
        CTAS for persistence
      
    

  






  Key Insights
  
    
      01
      Storage layout determines query performance. Row-based formats (CSV, JSON) store each record together: good for returning full rows, slow for scanning one column across millions. Columnar formats store each column together: excellent for analytical aggregations, less ideal for full-row retrieval.
    
    
      02
      Parquet is a hybrid: rows are partitioned into large row groups, and within each group data is stored columnarly. This gives both compression efficiency and fast columnar scans. The footer stores schema and per-row-group statistics enabling predicate pushdown: row groups are skipped before any data is read.
    
    
      03
      Parquet compresses dramatically better than CSV or JSON due to columnar grouping (similar values together) and dictionary encoding (repeating strings replaced with compact integer codes). The same dataset can be 3–10× smaller as Parquet, which means faster queries because less data is read from disk.
    
    
      04
      DuckDB is an in-process analytical SQL database, install as a package, query immediately. A database is a single .duckdb file. It uses vectorized, columnar execution and processes data in batches of 2,048 values, making it fast for aggregation-heavy analytical queries.
    
    
      05
      Once ingested into DuckDB, the source format is irrelevant. DuckDB normalizes CSV, Parquet, and JSON into its own internal columnar representation. Query performance converges regardless of source. Parquet is still the best source format because it ingests faster and the file is smaller on disk.
    
    
      06
      Native DuckDB vs. direct Parquet is a genuine trade-off. Native DuckDB is faster for coordination-heavy queries across many files (up to 3.8× on COUNT). Direct Parquet can win on narrow column scans due to its smaller footprint. Native DuckDB takes 3× more storage. Choose based on query frequency and storage constraints.

title: SQL, Keys, Indexes, and B+ Trees
text:
● SQL Database Essentials
    SQL, Keys,Indexes &B+ Trees
    What is SQL? What are its primary features? How does it Index? How does a tree structures power billions of queries per second.
    
      SQL Foundations
      Keys & Constraints
      Index Structures
      B+ Tree Internals
    
    Scroll to explore↓
  




  01
  
    Foundations
    What is SQL & how do tables work?
    SQL is the language of relational databases. Instead of storing everything in one spreadsheet or file, SQL stores related pieces of information in separate tables. Queries combine those tables only when needed.
  





  
  

    
    
      
        students table
        



student_id
name
major
year




1001
Alex
CS
Sophomore


1002
Maria
Math
Junior


1003
Sam
CS
Sophomore


1004
Nina
Physics
Senior




        
SELECT name, major
FROM students
WHERE year = 'Sophomore';
        
      
    

    
    
      
        
          
            students
            PKstudent_id
            name
            major
            year
          
          
            student_id
            ↔
            links
          
          
            enrollments
            PKenroll_id
            FKstudent_id
            course_id
            grade
          
        
        



enroll_id
student_id
course_id




E001
1001
CS301


E002
1001
CS401


E003
1003
CS301




      
    


    
      
        Defining keys in SQL
        
CREATE TABLE students (
  student_id  INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name        VARCHAR(100),
  major      VARCHAR(100)
);
CREATE TABLE enrollments (
  enrollment_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  student_id    INT,
  course_id    INT,
  FOREIGN KEY (student_id)
    REFERENCES students(student_id)
);
-- Add a primary key after creation:
ALTER TABLE students
ADD PRIMARY KEY (student_id);
-- Add a foreign key after creation:
ALTER TABLE enrollments
ADD CONSTRAINT fk_student
FOREIGN KEY (student_id)
REFERENCES students(student_id);
        
        
          
            PRIMARY KEY
            Defined at table creation or added later with ALTER TABLE
          
          
            FOREIGN KEY
            Defined at table creation or added later with ADD CONSTRAINT
          
        
      
    

  

  
  

    
      1.1 · SQL Foundations
      SQL is declarative and remote
      SQL (Structured Query Language) lets you create, query, update, and manage relational databases. A relational database stores data in tables — rows are records, columns are attributes/variables.
      The key insight: SQL is declarative. You specify what data you want; the database engine's optimizer figures out how to retrieve it efficiently. This is why the same SQL query can run substantially faster with an index than without.
      SQL queries are sent to a server (SQL server) where the database is stored, though this server is often remote, that doesn't have to be the case. SQL handles everything in executing the query and returns the resulting data to the user.
      On the left is an example of a SQL query on the table students that retreives the name and major of Sophmore students in the table.
      
        Declarative
        Relational
        Structured
        Query Language
      
    

    
      1.2 · Tables & Relationships
      Relational aspect of SQL and Keys
      A table represents an entity — students, courses, orders. Each row is one record; each column is one attribute. A primary key uniquely identifies each row.
      A primary key must be unique and never NULL. Most tables have one primary key, though composite keys (multiple columns) are valid and identify rows through a composition of multiple columns.
      Relational databases become powerful when multiple tables are connected. To do this: A foreign key is a column that references another table's primary key. A foreign key doesn't have to be made up of unique values but it does need to reference a primary key (which is made up of unique values). It enforces referential integrity — you cannot enroll a student who doesn't exist. The database prevents the inconsistency at the constraint level.
      The student_id column bridges the students and enrollments tables which enables powerful joins without redundancy. A foreign key defines and enforces a relationship between tables.
      
        Entities
        Attributes
        No Duplication
        Joins
      
    

    
      1.3 · Primary & Foreign Keys
      How to create keys in context
      On the left is the SQL command for creating primary and foreign keys in a SQL database.
      The CREATE TABLE command is used when one creates the key at the same time as creating the SQL table itself.
      The ALTER TABLE command is used when the table was already created but has no pre-defined key. For a primary key within the query one would use ADD PRIMARY KEY and for a foreign key within the query one would use ADD CONSTRAINT and must specify the constraint is a foreign key and the primary key that the foreign key references.
      Common confusion: a foreign key is simply a column in your table. The relationship is established from by declaring it as a reference to another table's primary key with a FOREIGN KEY constraint.
      
        Unique
        Not NULL
        Referential Integrity
        Composite Keys
      
    

  




  
    Key insight
    Keys define relationships. Indexes make those relationships fast to query.
    A primary key is a logical constraint (a column). An index is the underlying data structure the database mainatains to turn a key lookup from O(n) into O(log n). They coexist but are fundamentally different things.
  




  02
  
    Performance Structures
    Indexes
    An index is a separate ordered data structure that helps the database find rows faster by storing indexed column values and references to the corresponding table rows.
  





  

    
    
      
        Physical rows vs. Index order
        
          
            Physical table
            Row 1 Tom
            Row 2 Alex
            Row 4 Nina
            Row 7 Maria
            Row 9 Sam
          
          →
          
            Index on name
            Alex → Row 2
            Maria → Row 7
            Nina → Row 4
            Sam → Row 9
            Tom → Row 1
          
        
        
CREATE INDEX idx_name
  ON students(name);
CREATE INDEX idx_enroll_student
  ON enrollments(student_id);
        
      
    

    
    
      
        ❌ Full table scan (no index)
        
        Every row checked. Complexity: O(n).
        ✅ Index lookup
        
          Root
          →
          Branch
          →
          Leaf
          →
          Match!
        
        
          
            O(n)
            Full scan
          
          
            O(log n)
            Index lookup
          
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Index tradeoffs
        
          
            ✅ Reads
            Faster WHEREFaster JOINsEfficient ORDER BYFast range queries
          
          
            ⚠️ Writes
            Slower INSERTSlower UPDATESlower DELETEExtra storage
          
        
        
        Selectivity matters
        
          
            email = 'x@example.com'High ✓
            
            Matches ~1 row of millions
          
          
            status = 'active'Low ✗
            
            Matches 60% of rows — index barely helps
          
          
            country = 'US'Medium
            
            Depends on data distribution
          
        
      
    

  

  

    
      2.1 · What is an Index
      An index is a sorted, separate access structure
      An index is a separate data structure — like a phonebook or a textbook's back index — that helps the database find rows without reading every single one.
      The physical rows in a table can be in any order. An index stores keys in sorted logical order with references (row pointers) to where those rows actually live. The table itself doesn't move.
      Important: a key is a logical constraint (unique, not null). An index is a physical performance structure. Primary keys usually get an index automatically in MySQL/InnoDB — but the concepts are distinct.
      
        Sorted Keys
        Row References
        Separate Structure
        Faster Lookup
      
    

    
      2.2 · Why Indexes Help
      Advantage: Prevents scanning every row, by following a guided path
      Without an index on email, finding WHERE email = 'x@example.com' requires inspecting every row — a full table scan. With millions of rows this leads to computing inefficiencies and prolonged runtimes.
      With an index, the database follows an ordered tree path — root → branch → leaf — touching only a handful of nodes to land on the match. Essentially, an index cuts down the total number of comparisons needed to identify a row. This is the difference between O(n), which is the runtime without an index, and O(log n) which is the decreased runtime through an index.
      Indexes are most impactful for WHERE filters, JOIN conditions, ORDER BY sorting, and range queries like BETWEEN. Below the structure and functionality of an index is explained in depth.
      
        WHERE clauses
        JOIN conditions
        ORDER BY
        Range queries
      
    

    
      2.3 · Limitations & Tradeoffs
      Indexes not always fast and beneficial
      Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes. Every INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE must also update every relevant index which causes the structure of the index to alter. Too many indexes increase maintenance cost and storage of the database.
      Selectivity is critical. An index on a column with few distinct values (like a boolean is_active or status) may not help — too many rows match, and the database may choose a full scan anyway since that may be more efficient.
       Although primary keys must be unique by definition, a regular index (created with CREATE INDEX) has no uniqueness requirement at all. Sometimes, regular indexes can be helpful to speed up row retrieval and querying even if they aren't for primary keys. It is still important to consider selectivity or uniqueness when creating a regular index, as an index on a column with low selectivity may not provide performance benefits and can even degrade performance due to the overhead of maintaining the index and the potential for increased table lookups.
        
      Even with a perfect index, a query can still be slow if it matches many rows (leaf scanning) or those rows are scattered across many storage pages and has to perform many table lookups. Table lookups happen when the database engine jumps from the index back to the main table to retrieve a full row of data after finding a match in the index. This is not a problem in MySQL because the table data lives directly in the primary key's B+ tree leaves (clustered index), so there's no second trip. Secondary indexes, however, (on non-primary-key columns) always require a second lookup back to the primary key tree to get the full row.
      
        Write overhead
        Low selectivity
        Leaf scanning
        Table lookups
      
    

  




  
    So what structure does an index use?
    Most database indexes are B trees. MySQL uses B+ trees which are a balanced tree structure designed for disk-based storage with high fanout and sorted leaf chains.
    Understanding the tree explains why indexes are fast for both equality lookups and range queries, and why they stay efficient even with millions of rows.
  




  03
  
    Data Structures
    B+ Trees
    The tree structure that makes indexes fast and optimized in MySQL.
  





  

    
    
      
        
          B+ Tree Structure
        
        
          
        
        
          Root node (top) → branch nodes (middle) → leaf nodes (bottom, linked in sorted order)
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Search path for key 57
        
          
            
              
                
              
              
                
              
            
            
            
            
            
            
            
            
            
            
            

            
            39 | 83 | 98
            
            < 39
            46|53|57|83
            83-98
            
            42|48|51
            55 | 57 | 57
            61|72|79
            
            
            KEY 57 FOUND ✓
          
        
        
          1Root [39|83|98]: 57 > 39 and < 83 → middle pointer
          2Branch [46|53|57|83]: 57 = 57 → leaf pointer
          3Leaf [55|57|57]: key found, return row reference
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Why B+ trees stay shallow
        
          Fanout of 100 → 3 levels covers
          1,000,000
          leaf entries with only 3 page reads
        
        
        
          
            ROOT
          
          ↓ × many pointers
          
            Br
            Br
            Br
            ...
          
          ↓ × many pointers
          
            Lf
            Lf
            Lf
            Lf
            ...
          
        
        
          
            Equality
            Root → leaf in O(log n)
          
          
            Range
            Walk sorted leaf chain
          
        
      
    

  

  

    
      3.1 · B+ Tree Structure
      Root, branches, leaves
      A B+ tree has three node types. The root at the top is the entry point for every search. Branch nodes in the middle store only keys and pointers — they guide the search but hold no row data. Leaf nodes at the bottom store the actual index entries and row references.
      In MySQL/InnoDB, the primary key index is clustered — the actual table row data lives in the leaf nodes of the primary key B+ tree. Secondary indexes store the indexed column value plus the corresponding primary key, requiring a second tree lookup to retrieve the full row.
      
        Root node
        Branch nodes
        Leaf nodes
        Clustered index
      
    

    
      3.2 · Leaf Chain
      Leaf nodes are linked for range scans
      One of the key advantages of B+ trees over regular binary trees: leaf nodes are linked left-to-right in sorted order. This dashed green chain in the diagram is what enables efficient range queries.
      To answer WHERE score BETWEEN 43 AND 67, the database traverses the tree to find 43, then simply walks the leaf chain to collect everything up to 67 — no re-traversal needed.
      
        Sorted chain
        Range scans
        BETWEEN
        ORDER BY
      
    

    
      3.3 · Tree Traversal
      Tree traversal walk-through
      Consider the task where the goal is to search for a key 57: start at the root [39|83|98]. Since 57 is between 39 and 83, follow the middle pointer. At branch [46|53|57|83], find the position of 57 and follow to the leaf [55|57|57]. Found.
      Notice that only one node is visited per level. This is always true for any key lookup in a B+ tree, regardless of the key or the size of the data. At each node, the search key is compared against the stored keys and exactly one pointer is followed downward. The result is that the number of nodes inspected is always exactly equal to the depth of the tree. This is where the O(log n) runtime comes from: each level cuts the search space by the branching factor, and the tree's depth grows only logarithmically with the number of rows.
      
        O(log n)
        One path
        Exact lookup
      
    

    
      3.4 · Efficiency & Fanout
      Shallow trees handle millions of rows in 3–4 reads
      Each node in a B+ tree corresponds to one page (typically 16 KB in MySQL/InnoDB). Because a page can hold hundreds of keys, the fanout — the number of entries per node — is very high.
      With fanout 100: level 1 (root) covers 100 branches. Level 2 covers 10,000. Level 3 covers 1,000,000 leaves. Therefore, a tree covering a million rows reads only 3 pages for any lookup. That's why B+ trees scale so well.
      The tree is always balanced — all leaf nodes sit at the same depth — because insertions and deletions trigger automatic node splits and merges that propagate up the tree to maintain this property. This guarantees predictable search time regardless of which key you look up.
      
        Efficient
        Always balanced
        Predictable
      
    


 
      3.4 · Efficiency & Fanout
      Index structures differ across SQL dialects
      MySQL primarily uses B+ trees to structure its indexes, but other dialects treat indexing slightly differently. For example, PostgreSQL and SQLite default to B tree indexing, where (among other differences) row data is stored in all levels of the tree rather than just in the leaves. In Postgres, it is also possible to manually choose alternative indexing strategies like hash (simple and efficient for equality comparisons) or GiST operating classes (for geometric data and nearest-neighbor queries). Generally, though, understanding the structure of B and B+ trees is essential to creating and working with indexes in SQL.  
      
        B vs. B+ trees
        Manual index structure selection
      
    

  




  04
  
    Best Practices
    Practical indexing: What to take away from indexing
    Knowing the theory behind indexes can be helpful but only if you know how to utilize the information for your own good.
  





  

    
    
      
        Composite index: (customer_id, order_date)
        
          Sorted by customer_id first, then order_date within each customer:
          cust: 12024-01-05
          cust: 12024-03-12
          cust: 12024-07-01
          cust: 22024-02-18
          cust: 22024-09-30
          · · ·

          
            cust: 10
            2024-01-09 ← found
          
        
        
CREATE INDEX idx_cust_date
  ON orders(customer_id, order_date);
EXPLAIN
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE customer_id = 10
ORDER BY order_date;
        
      
    

    
    
      

        
        
          SQL indexes: OLTP vs OLAP
        

        
        

          
          
            
              ✅ OLTP
            
            Transactional
            
              Finds few specific rows
              B+ tree skips most data
              Joins on foreign keys
              WHERE, lookup, update
            
            O(log n) and index beneficial
          

          
          
            
              ⚠️ OLAP
            
            Analytical
            
              Scans many/all rows
              Index can't help a full scan
              Aggregates whole columns
              AVG, SUM, GROUP BY
            
            O(n) and index not beneficial
          

        

        
        
        
          
            Use SQL + B+ tree indexes for transactional row lookups.
            Use DuckDB / Snowflake for analytical column scans.
          
        

      
    

  

  

    
      4.1 · Composite Indexes
      Column order (during creation) in composite indexes is critical
      A composite index on (customer_id, order_date) sorts first by customer_id, then by order_date within each customer group. This makes queries that filter by customer_id alone very efficient.
      It also helps queries filtering by both columns, and sorting by order_date after filtering by customer_id. However, a query filtering only by order_date won't benefit much — the index isn't sorted by that column alone.
      What to take away: put the column you filter on most frequently first when creating a composite index. Doing so improves both lookup speed and query efficiency compared to any other column ordering.
      
        Leading column first
        Prefix matching
        Column order matters
      
    

    
      4.2 · Guidelines
      Indexes make SQL fast for row-based, transactional queries
      SQL indexes are purpose-built for OLTP — Online Transactional Processing. These are queries that locate and return a small number of specific rows: find one customer, update one order, look up one student. The B+ tree traverses from root to leaf in O(log n) and lands on exactly the rows you need, touching almost nothing else in the table.
      This is fundamentally different from analytical queries — the kind that ask "what is the average grade across all students?" or "how many orders were placed per month this year?" Those queries need to scan many or all rows and aggregate across entire columns. An index does not help here because there is no shortcut — every row must be read regardless. This workload is called OLAP (Online Analytical Processing).
      SQL finds the right rows fast through its indexes, but analytical queries need to read one column across millions of rows meaning the database still has to touch every row's storage page. This makes analytical queries less efficient in SQL compared to transactional queries (row-based). However, based on the index model of SQL, SQL is especially efficient and strong for row-based queries.
      The practical takeaway: use SQL and its B+ tree indexes for transactional workloads like precise lookups, joins on foreign keys, filtering by specific values. For large-scale analytical work across many rows and few columns, a column-oriented system is the better architectural choice (like DuckDB or Snowflake).
      
        OLTP
        Row lookup
        OLAP
        Column scan
      
    

  




  Key Takeaways
  
    
      01
      SQL is a declarative language, a query is sent to a remote SQL server, and the optimizer decides how to retrieve it efficiently. Rows are records, columns are attributes.
    
    
      02
      Primary keys uniquely identify rows and must be unique and never NULL. Foreign keys are columns that reference another table's primary key. They create relationships and enforce referential integrity. Both can be defined at table creation or added later with ALTER TABLE.
    
    
      03
      Indexes are separate physical structures, distinct from keys, that store column values in sorted order with row references. They reduce lookups from O(n) full scans to O(log n) guided tree traversal. Composite indexes require putting the most-filtered column first for maximum efficiency.
    
    
      04
      B+ trees power MySQL indexes. The root is the entry point, branch nodes guide the search, and leaf nodes store the data. Leaf nodes are linked in sorted order enabling fast range scans. With high fanout, just 3–4 levels can cover millions of rows.
    
    
      05
      Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes — every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE must also update the index structure. Low-selectivity columns (like booleans or status fields) benefit little from indexing since too many rows match.
    
    
      06
      SQL indexes excel at OLTP, precise row lookups, joins on foreign keys, transactional queries. For OLAP analytical workloads that scan entire columns across millions of rows, column-oriented systems like DuckDB or Snowflake are the better architectural choice.

title: Pivot Types: Reshaping vs Cross-Tabulation
text:
The word “pivot” covers two different operations:

Pure reshaping — changing the shape of data (long → wide) without performing any arithmetic on values. Data is redistributed into new columns; nothing is summarized or collapsed.
Cross-tabulation — reshaping plus aggregation. Multiple source rows sharing the same (row-group, column-group) combination are collapsed into a single cell via a summary function (mean, count, sum, etc.).

We’ll demonstrate both using the gapminder dataset, which records country-level statistics at five-year intervals from 1952 to 2007. The variables we’ll use are:

country — the unit of observation
continent — the geographic grouping
year — the time point (1952, 1957, …, 2007)
lifeExp — life expectancy at birth (years)



Show the code
library(gapminder)
library(tidyverse)
library(DBI)
library(duckdb)
library(RPostgres)

data(gapminder)
glimpse(gapminder)


Rows: 1,704
Columns: 6
$ country   <fct> "Afghanistan", "Afghanistan", "Afghanistan", "Afghanistan", …
$ continent <fct> Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, …
$ year      <int> 1952, 1957, 1962, 1967, 1972, 1977, 1982, 1987, 1992, 1997, …
$ lifeExp   <dbl> 28.801, 30.332, 31.997, 34.020, 36.088, 38.438, 39.854, 40.8…
$ pop       <int> 8425333, 9240934, 10267083, 11537966, 13079460, 14880372, 12…
$ gdpPercap <dbl> 779.4453, 820.8530, 853.1007, 836.1971, 739.9811, 786.1134, …

title: Pivot Types: Reshaping vs Cross-Tabulation
text:
The word “pivot” covers two different operations:

Pure reshaping — changing the shape of data (long → wide) without performing any arithmetic on values. Data is redistributed into new columns; nothing is summarized or collapsed.
Cross-tabulation — reshaping plus aggregation. Multiple source rows sharing the same (row-group, column-group) combination are collapsed into a single cell via a summary function (mean, count, sum, etc.).

We’ll demonstrate both using the gapminder dataset, which records country-level statistics at five-year intervals from 1952 to 2007. The variables we’ll use are:

country — the unit of observation
continent — the geographic grouping
year — the time point (1952, 1957, …, 2007)
lifeExp — life expectancy at birth (years)



Show the code
library(gapminder)
library(tidyverse)
library(DBI)
library(duckdb)
library(RPostgres)

data(gapminder)
glimpse(gapminder)


Rows: 1,704
Columns: 6
$ country   <fct> "Afghanistan", "Afghanistan", "Afghanistan", "Afghanistan", …
$ continent <fct> Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, …
$ year      <int> 1952, 1957, 1962, 1967, 1972, 1977, 1982, 1987, 1992, 1997, …
$ lifeExp   <dbl> 28.801, 30.332, 31.997, 34.020, 36.088, 38.438, 39.854, 40.8…
$ pop       <int> 8425333, 9240934, 10267083, 11537966, 13079460, 14880372, 12…
$ gdpPercap <dbl> 779.4453, 820.8530, 853.1007, 836.1971, 739.9811, 786.1134, …

title: Pivot Types: Reshaping vs Cross-Tabulation
text:
Part 1: Pure Reshaping

The data structure
For pure reshaping, the requirement is that each combination of row-identifier and spreading-column value appears exactly once in the source data. Thus there is nothing to aggregate, only redistribute.
In our first example:

Rows: country
Columns: year (each distinct year becomes a column header)
Cells: life expectancy

Each country has exactly one life expectancy per year, so no aggregation is needed or possible. This is the defining condition for pure reshaping.


Show the code
# Confirm each country-year combination is unique
gapminder |>
  count(country, year) |>
  filter(n > 1)


# A tibble: 0 × 3
# ℹ 3 variables: country <fct>, year <int>, n <int>


No rows — every country-year combination is unique. Pure reshaping is appropriate here.


Approach 1: pivot_wider() in R (tidyverse)
pivot_wider() is the canonical R function for pure reshaping. It takes three arguments that map directly to the three pivot roles:

id_cols — the row-grouping specification (country)
names_from — the spreading dimension (year)
values_from — the measure (no aggregation, just redistribution of lifeExp)



Show the code
wide_tidyverse <- gapminder |>
  select(country, year, lifeExp) |>
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols     = country,
    names_from  = year,
    values_from = lifeExp
  )

wide_tidyverse


# A tibble: 142 × 13
   country `1952` `1957` `1962` `1967` `1972` `1977` `1982` `1987` `1992` `1997`
   <fct>    <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>
 1 Afghan…   28.8   30.3   32.0   34.0   36.1   38.4   39.9   40.8   41.7   41.8
 2 Albania   55.2   59.3   64.8   66.2   67.7   68.9   70.4   72     71.6   73.0
 3 Algeria   43.1   45.7   48.3   51.4   54.5   58.0   61.4   65.8   67.7   69.2
 4 Angola    30.0   32.0   34     36.0   37.9   39.5   39.9   39.9   40.6   41.0
 5 Argent…   62.5   64.4   65.1   65.6   67.1   68.5   69.9   70.8   71.9   73.3
 6 Austra…   69.1   70.3   70.9   71.1   71.9   73.5   74.7   76.3   77.6   78.8
 7 Austria   66.8   67.5   69.5   70.1   70.6   72.2   73.2   74.9   76.0   77.5
 8 Bahrain   50.9   53.8   56.9   59.9   63.3   65.6   69.1   70.8   72.6   73.9
 9 Bangla…   37.5   39.3   41.2   43.5   45.3   46.9   50.0   52.8   56.0   59.4
10 Belgium   68     69.2   70.2   70.9   71.4   72.8   73.9   75.4   76.5   77.5
# ℹ 132 more rows
# ℹ 2 more variables: `2002` <dbl>, `2007` <dbl>


Notice the column names (1952, 1957, …, 2007) came directly from the distinct values in the year column which is the defining feature of any pivot.


Approach 2: DuckDB PIVOT (without USING)
DuckDB’s PIVOT command without a USING clause performs pure reshaping. This is equivalent to pivot_wider(). No aggregation is specified; values are simply redistributed. The output below only shows 6 rows.


Show the code
# Set up DuckDB and load the data
con_duck <- dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb())
dbWriteTable(con_duck, "gapminder", gapminder, overwrite = TRUE)

wide_duckdb <- dbGetQuery(con_duck, "
  PIVOT (SELECT country, year, lifeExp FROM gapminder)
  ON year
  USING first(lifeExp)
")

# Sort to match tidyverse output for comparison
wide_duckdb <- wide_duckdb |> arrange(country)

head(wide_duckdb, 6)


      country   1952   1957   1962   1967   1972   1977   1982   1987   1992
1 Afghanistan 28.801 30.332 31.997 34.020 36.088 38.438 39.854 40.822 41.674
2     Albania 55.230 59.280 64.820 66.220 67.690 68.930 70.420 72.000 71.581
3     Algeria 43.077 45.685 48.303 51.407 54.518 58.014 61.368 65.799 67.744
4      Angola 30.015 31.999 34.000 35.985 37.928 39.483 39.942 39.906 40.647
5   Argentina 62.485 64.399 65.142 65.634 67.065 68.481 69.942 70.774 71.868
6   Australia 69.120 70.330 70.930 71.100 71.930 73.490 74.740 76.320 77.560
    1997   2002   2007
1 41.763 42.129 43.828
2 72.950 75.651 76.423
3 69.152 70.994 72.301
4 40.963 41.003 42.731
5 73.275 74.340 75.320
6 78.830 80.370 81.235


Note: even though USING first(lifeExp) looks like an aggregation, since each country-year combination is unique, first() always operates on exactly one value and simply returns it unchanged. The output is structurally identical to the pivot_wider() result, except that this output is not rounded like in Approach 1.


Approach 3: Manual reshaping in DuckDB (CASE WHEN)
The manual approach makes every column fully explicit, with no built-in pivot mechanism involved at all.


Show the code
wide_manual <- dbGetQuery(con_duck, "
  SELECT
    country,
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1952 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1952\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1957 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1957\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1962 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1962\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1967 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1967\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1972 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1972\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1977 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1977\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1982 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1982\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1987 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1987\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1992 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1992\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1997 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1997\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 2002 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"2002\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 2007 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"2007\"
  FROM gapminder
  GROUP BY country
  ORDER BY country
")

head(wide_manual, 6)


      country   1952   1957   1962   1967   1972   1977   1982   1987   1992
1 Afghanistan 28.801 30.332 31.997 34.020 36.088 38.438 39.854 40.822 41.674
2     Albania 55.230 59.280 64.820 66.220 67.690 68.930 70.420 72.000 71.581
3     Algeria 43.077 45.685 48.303 51.407 54.518 58.014 61.368 65.799 67.744
4      Angola 30.015 31.999 34.000 35.985 37.928 39.483 39.942 39.906 40.647
5   Argentina 62.485 64.399 65.142 65.634 67.065 68.481 69.942 70.774 71.868
6   Australia 69.120 70.330 70.930 71.100 71.930 73.490 74.740 76.320 77.560
    1997   2002   2007
1 41.763 42.129 43.828
2 72.950 75.651 76.423
3 69.152 70.994 72.301
4 40.963 41.003 42.731
5 73.275 74.340 75.320
6 78.830 80.370 81.235


MAX() is used here not to aggregate multiple values, but because GROUP BY country requires an aggregate function around non-grouped columns, and since each group contains exactly one row per year, MAX() simply returns that single value.


Verifying all three outputs match


Show the code
# Align column types and order for comparison
tidy_sorted <- wide_tidyverse |>
  arrange(country) |>
  mutate(across(-country, as.numeric))

duck_sorted <- wide_duckdb |>
  arrange(country) |>
  rename_with(~ str_remove(.x, "^lifeExp_"), -country) |>
  mutate(across(-country, as.numeric))

manual_sorted <- wide_manual |>
  arrange(country) |>
  mutate(across(-country, as.numeric))

all.equal(tidy_sorted, duck_sorted,   check.attributes = FALSE)


[1] TRUE


Show the code
all.equal(tidy_sorted, manual_sorted, check.attributes = FALSE)


[1] TRUE


All three approaches produce identical results — the same countries, the same years as column headers, and the same life expectancy values in every cell.


Can crosstab() do pure reshaping?
Short answer: not cleanly. crosstab() always expects exactly one (row_id, category, value) triple per row of the source query, where the value has already been aggregated. In the pure reshaping case (one value per country-year), you can technically pass crosstab() a query that produces those triples althought it is a little more complicated than the previous options:


Show the code
con_local <- dbConnect(
  RPostgres::Postgres(),
  dbname   = "postgres",
  host     = "localhost",
  port     = 5432,
  user     = "sebastianpollack",
  password = "sup"
)




Show the code
# Requires a local PostgreSQL connection with tablefunc installed
dbExecute(con_local, "CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS tablefunc;")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbWriteTable(con_local, "gapminder", gapminder, overwrite = TRUE)

wide_crosstab <- dbGetQuery(con_local, '
  SELECT * FROM crosstab(
    \'SELECT country,
            year::text,
            "lifeExp"
     FROM gapminder
     ORDER BY country, year::text\',
    \'SELECT DISTINCT year::text FROM gapminder ORDER BY year::text\'
  ) AS ct(
    country text,
    "1952" float, "1957" float, "1962" float, "1967" float,
    "1972" float, "1977" float, "1982" float, "1987" float,
    "1992" float, "1997" float, "2002" float, "2007" float
  )
')

head(wide_crosstab,6)


      country   1952   1957   1962   1967   1972   1977   1982   1987   1992
1 Afghanistan 28.801 30.332 31.997 34.020 36.088 38.438 39.854 40.822 41.674
2     Albania 55.230 59.280 64.820 66.220 67.690 68.930 70.420 72.000 71.581
3     Algeria 43.077 45.685 48.303 51.407 54.518 58.014 61.368 65.799 67.744
4      Angola 30.015 31.999 34.000 35.985 37.928 39.483 39.942 39.906 40.647
5   Argentina 62.485 64.399 65.142 65.634 67.065 68.481 69.942 70.774 71.868
6   Australia 69.120 70.330 70.930 71.100 71.930 73.490 74.740 76.320 77.560
    1997   2002   2007
1 41.763 42.129 43.828
2 72.950 75.651 76.423
3 69.152 70.994 72.301
4 40.963 41.003 42.731
5 73.275 74.340 75.320
6 78.830 80.370 81.235


This will produce the same output — but notice that crosstab() is doing exactly what pivot_wider() and the manual CASE WHEN do: retrieving one existing value per cell. crosstab()’s mandatory output-column declaration makes it more verbose and more fragile than pivot_wider() for a pure reshaping task.

title: Pivot Types: Reshaping vs Cross-Tabulation
text:
Part 2: Cross-Tabulation

The data structure
Cross-tabulation is appropriate when multiple source rows share the same (row-group, column-group) combination, and those rows must be collapsed into a single cell via an aggregation function.
In our second example:

Rows: continent
Columns: year
Cells: average life expectancy across all countries in that continent-year combination

Each continent contains multiple countries, so each (continent, year) pair has multiple source rows — aggregation is not optional, it’s required.


Show the code
# How many countries per continent-year?
gapminder |>
  count(continent, year) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = year, values_from = n)


# A tibble: 5 × 13
  continent `1952` `1957` `1962` `1967` `1972` `1977` `1982` `1987` `1992`
  <fct>      <int>  <int>  <int>  <int>  <int>  <int>  <int>  <int>  <int>
1 Africa        52     52     52     52     52     52     52     52     52
2 Americas      25     25     25     25     25     25     25     25     25
3 Asia          33     33     33     33     33     33     33     33     33
4 Europe        30     30     30     30     30     30     30     30     30
5 Oceania        2      2      2      2      2      2      2      2      2
# ℹ 3 more variables: `1997` <int>, `2002` <int>, `2007` <int>




Approach 1: Manual (CASE WHEN + AVG)


Show the code
ct_manual <- dbGetQuery(con_duck, "
  SELECT
    continent,
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1952 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1952\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1957 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1957\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1962 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1962\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1967 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1967\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1972 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1972\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1977 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1977\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1982 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1982\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1987 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1987\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1992 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1992\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1997 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1997\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 2002 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"2002\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 2007 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"2007\"
  FROM gapminder
  GROUP BY continent
  ORDER BY continent
")

ct_manual


  continent  1952  1957  1962  1967  1972  1977  1982  1987  1992  1997  2002
1    Africa 39.14 41.27 43.32 45.33 47.45 49.58 51.59 53.34 53.63 53.60 53.33
2  Americas 53.28 55.96 58.40 60.41 62.39 64.39 66.23 68.09 69.57 71.15 72.42
3      Asia 46.31 49.32 51.56 54.66 57.32 59.61 62.62 64.85 66.54 68.02 69.23
4    Europe 64.41 66.70 68.54 69.74 70.78 71.94 72.81 73.64 74.44 75.51 76.70
5   Oceania 69.26 70.30 71.09 71.31 71.91 72.85 74.29 75.32 76.94 78.19 79.74
   2007
1 54.81
2 73.61
3 70.73
4 77.65
5 80.72


Here AVG() is taking the average of life expectancy per year for every continent.


Approach 2: DuckDB PIVOT with USING avg()


Show the code
ct_duckdb <- dbGetQuery(con_duck, "
  PIVOT (SELECT continent, year, lifeExp FROM gapminder)
  ON year
  USING ROUND(AVG(lifeExp), 2)
  GROUP BY continent
  ORDER BY continent
")

ct_duckdb


  continent  1952  1957  1962  1967  1972  1977  1982  1987  1992  1997  2002
1    Africa 39.14 41.27 43.32 45.33 47.45 49.58 51.59 53.34 53.63 53.60 53.33
2  Americas 53.28 55.96 58.40 60.41 62.39 64.39 66.23 68.09 69.57 71.15 72.42
3      Asia 46.31 49.32 51.56 54.66 57.32 59.61 62.62 64.85 66.54 68.02 69.23
4    Europe 64.41 66.70 68.54 69.74 70.78 71.94 72.81 73.64 74.44 75.51 76.70
5   Oceania 69.26 70.30 71.09 71.31 71.91 72.85 74.29 75.32 76.94 78.19 79.74
   2007
1 54.81
2 73.61
3 70.73
4 77.65
5 80.72


The USING avg(lifeExp) clause is now performing averaging life expectancy given the specified conditions and formatting.


Approach 3: crosstab() in PostgreSQL


Show the code
dbWriteTable(con_local, "gapminder", gapminder, overwrite = TRUE)

dbGetQuery(con_local, "
  SELECT * FROM crosstab(
    'SELECT continent,
            year::text,
            ROUND(AVG(\"lifeExp\")::numeric, 2)
     FROM gapminder
     GROUP BY continent, year
     ORDER BY continent, year::text',
    'SELECT DISTINCT year::text FROM gapminder ORDER BY year::text'
  ) AS ct(
    continent text,
    \"1952\" numeric, \"1957\" numeric, \"1962\" numeric, \"1967\" numeric,
    \"1972\" numeric, \"1977\" numeric, \"1982\" numeric, \"1987\" numeric,
    \"1992\" numeric, \"1997\" numeric, \"2002\" numeric, \"2007\" numeric
  )
")


This is crosstab()’s main use case: the source query performs the GROUP BY continent, year aggregation first, producing one pre-aggregated (continent, year, avg_lifeExp) triple per row, which is the three-column structure crosstab() requires. It then reshapes those triples into the wide format.


Verifying manual and DuckDB PIVOT match


Show the code
manual_sorted_ct <- ct_manual |> arrange(continent)
duck_sorted_ct   <- ct_duckdb |> arrange(continent)

all.equal(manual_sorted_ct, duck_sorted_ct, check.attributes = FALSE)


[1] TRUE

title: Pivot Types: Reshaping vs Cross-Tabulation
text:
Summary









Pure Reshaping
Cross-Tabulation




Row count changes?
No
Yes (fewer rows out)


Aggregation required?
No
Yes


R tidyverse
pivot_wider()
pivot_wider() + pre-aggregation


DuckDB PIVOT
PIVOT ... ON ... USING first()
PIVOT ... ON ... USING avg()


Manual SQL
MAX(CASE WHEN ...) + GROUP BY
AVG(CASE WHEN ...) + GROUP BY


PostgreSQL crosstab()
Works but awkward, no native mode for it
Natural fit, designed for pre-aggregated input

title: sql4stats
text: Welcome to the SQL for Statistics site! This site is designed to help you learn SQL in the context of statistical analysis. Here, you’ll find tutorials, examples, and resources that will guide you through using SQL to manipulate and analyze data effectively. Whether you’re a beginner or looking to enhance your SQL skills for statistical applications, this site has something for you.

title: SQL Joins
text: When executing a SQL query, joins operate on two distinct levels: the logical join (what you define in SQL syntax) and the physical join (how the query optimizer actually executes it behind the scenes). Understanding both layers is important — it can help you write more efficient queries, reduce computation costs, and diagnose performance bottlenecks.

title: SQL Joins
text:
Nested Loop Join
The nested loop join is the simplest physical strategy to understand. For simplicity, we will focus on two-table inner joins. First, assume neither table is sorted. First, the smaller table is designated as the “outer” table and the larger table as “inner”. Staying at a fixed row in the outer table, the engine performs a sequential scan on the inner table until it finds a match, records that match, moves onto the next row of the outer table, scans the entire inner table for matches, so on and so forth. This is why nested loop joins can cause catastrophically long runtimes with unsorted input tables – if table A has m rows and table B has n rows, the worst-case time complexity is O(m*n). However, you can improve performance by indexing or sorting the inner table so that the engine no longer has to scan the entire inner table on each pass (the search can end early once all instances of the join key are found).

title: SQL Joins
text:
Merge (Sort-Merge) Join
A merge join is one of the most efficient joins — assuming the two datasets you want to join are pre-sorted. However, this requirement can cause performance issues since sorting the data first can drastically impact overall computation. Additionally, merge joins require at least one equijoin predicate. The algorithm works by maintaining a pointer on each sorted dataset and traversing both in the same direction simultaneously. As matches are found, they are stored. Once there isn’t a match, the pointer at the smallest value advances. This continues until all rows have been processed.
The sort-merge process itself breaks into two phases:

Sort
The dataset is split into runs that fit in memory, each run is sorted individually, and the sorted blocks are written back to disk. Runs can be sorted by indexes or explicit sorting algorithms (indexes are often faster).


Merge – the sorted runs are combined into a final joined result.
A key advantage over nested loops is its cost profile: unlike the nested loops join where the total cost may be proportional to the product of the number of rows in the input tables, with a merge join each table is read at most once and the total cost is proportional to the sum of the number of rows in the inputs. It’s worth noting that performance may vary when dealing with one-to-many or many-to-many relationships, and there is additional nuance in the approach for many-to-many situations.

title: SQL Joins
text:
Hash Join
To understand the hash join, it’s first important to know what a hash table is. Essentially, a hash table is a data structure that stores information in key-value pairs for more efficient lookups. It is an array of a predetermined size, where a small, representative piece of information (key) “hashes” (corresponds) to a specific index in the array. To find specific values within the data, then, you only need to hash the key to extract the relevant information. A hash join relies on hashing and operates in two distinct phases: the build phase and the probe phase.

Build Phase
The typically smaller of the two tables (the “build” input) is scanned, and a hash table is constructed using the values of the join key. Each entry stores a tuple from the build input along with its hash value, distributing data evenly across different “buckets.”


Probe Phase
The larger table (the “probe” input) is scanned next. The join key is hashed using the same function as in the build phase for each tuple. The hash value is then used to check the corresponding bucket in the hash table for matches. If a match is found, the tuples from both inputs are combined.
Hash Collisions are a potential edge case to be aware of. A hash collision occurs when two or more distinct join key values hash to the same bucket. When a collision happens, the system must check each value in the bucket to determine if a true match exists. Depending on the number of collisions and bucket size, this can slow down the join operation — however, collisions are typically kept to a minimum with a well-distributed hash function.
Hash joins are commonly used for larger table joins, as the algorithm’s time complexity is linear at O(N + M).
